answer_md:
# Confidence/provenance scoring in machine-mined decision & knowledge records

Four real, mechanics-documented examples beyond Lore (arXiv:2603.15566), covering the two axes
asked for: (1) does confidence rise over time with corroboration, and (2) what happens when later
evidence contradicts an earlier record.

---

## 1. NELL — Never-Ending Language Learning (CMU, running since 2010)

Source: [Wikipedia summary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Never-Ending_Language_Learning), primary
papers via [AAAI](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/9498) and
[cdn.aaai.org PDF](https://cdn.aaai.org/ojs/7519/7519-13-11049-1-2-20201228.pdf).

NELL reads the web continuously and maintains a knowledge base of "confidence-weighted beliefs"
(120M+ by 2018). Facts start as **candidates** and are **promoted to beliefs** once confidence
crosses a documented threshold (~0.9), computed from multiple coupled learning modules (different
extraction methods) agreeing.

1. **Rises over time?** Yes, functionally. Confidence for a candidate fact is recomputed across
   NELL's iterative learning cycles as more of its ~4,100 coupled learning tasks independently
   extract corroborating instances — this is the whole point of "coupled" learning (multiple
   weak learners voting up a shared belief over many iterations, not a single one-shot score).
   Tom Mitchell is quoted describing the system as one that "self-corrects when it has more
   information, as it learns more."
2. **Handles contradiction?** Only partially, and largely via **human curation, not an automatic
   mechanism**. NELL famously accumulated absurd beliefs (e.g. classifying both "cookies" and
   computer files as baked goods) that persisted until CMU researchers manually pruned them "every
   few weeks." There is no documented automatic demotion/flagging when a later extraction
   contradicts an already-promoted belief — promotion is a one-way ratchet reinforced by
   corroboration, and contradiction cleanup is a manual sweep, not a derived signal. Stuart
   Russell's 2019 critique notes NELL has "confidence in only 3%" of its total beliefs — most
   candidates simply never accumulate enough corroboration to promote.

**Verdict:** confidence rises via corroboration (yes) / contradiction handling is manual curation,
not automatic (weak/no on the second axis).

---

## 2. Kantara / DRMiner — Rationale & Decision Graphs mined from commits (closest analogue to Lore)

Sources: original Kantara framework, Dhaouadi et al., ["End-to-End Rationale
 Reconstruction"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.00398) (ASE '22); LLM instantiation,
["Automated Extraction and Analysis of Developer's Rationale in Open Source Software"](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11005) (arXiv:2506.11005).

This is the **most directly comparable prior art to dark-factory's KDD/Lisa use case**: it mines
design *decisions* and *rationale* out of commit messages (not live human ADR authoring) into a
knowledge graph with explicit typed edges between decisions: `history` (a decision evolves an
earlier one), `similar`, and — critically — **`contradicts`**.

1. **Rises over time?** No. Extraction is **static and one-shot**: an LLM (ChatGPT in the 2506.11005
   instantiation) extracts a Decision/Rationale tuple once per commit/sentence with "no iterative
   refinement based on later commits." There is no confidence-accumulation mechanism as more
   commits pile up — quality issues ("some extracted tuples do not make sense or contain noise")
   are treated as one-time extraction noise, not something a later corroboration pass fixes.
2. **Handles contradiction?** Yes — this is the one system in this survey with a **purpose-built,
   automatic contradiction detector**, but it flags rather than resolves. The 2506.11005 paper
   computes a `contradicts` relationship between every pair of the 527 extracted decisions using an
   NLI model (roberta-large-mnli) with a confidence threshold of 0.9, finding 396 automatic
   contradiction links. The mechanism is explicitly two-stage: direct decision-vs-decision
   contradiction, plus an indirect check ("look for decisions similar to the new decision, then for
   decisions contradictory with those similar decisions"). **No automatic superseding or confidence
   drop occurs** — the paper is explicit that this "should help developers verify" the decisions;
   resolution is left to human review, not automated.

**Verdict:** confidence is static per-extraction (no rise) / contradiction is automatically
**detected and flagged** via a typed graph edge, but resolution (supersede/drop confidence) is
manual, not automatic.

---

## 3. Zep / Graphiti — temporal knowledge graph for AI agent memory

Source: [arXiv:2501.13956, "Zep: A Temporal Knowledge Graph Architecture for Agent Memory"](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.13956v1).

Zep builds a graph of facts ("edges") extracted from agent conversations, with a **bi-temporal**
model: each edge carries both an event timeline (`t_valid`/`t_invalid` — when the fact was/stopped
being true in the world) and a transactional timeline (`t'_created`/`t'_expired` — when Zep itself
learned/superseded it).

1. **Rises over time?** Not really — and this is a useful negative data point. The paper's own
   language does mention edges being "updated either by inserting new relations, updating
   confidence or context, or invalidating prior edges if superseded," implying a confidence field
   exists, but the paper does **not document a mechanism where confidence numerically increases as
   more corroborating mentions accumulate**. Fact resolution in Graphiti is essentially binary:
   dedupe (same edge, no change) or invalidate (contradicted, close the validity window) — there is
   no described "seen N times → score climbs" curve like NELL's.
2. **Handles contradiction?** Yes, and this is Zep's standout mechanic, precise and fully automatic:
   an LLM compares each new edge against semantically related existing edges; when it identifies a
   **temporally overlapping contradiction**, it "invalidates the affected edges by setting their
   `t_invalid` to the `t_valid` of the invalidating edge." Nothing is deleted — the old fact's
   validity window is closed and the new fact is inserted alongside it, with the transactional
   timeline used to determine precedence ("Graphiti consistently prioritizes new information when
   determining edge invalidation"). This gives a full audit trail of *what was believed true when*,
   which is a stronger provenance story than a bare confidence number.

**Verdict:** no documented confidence-rise-by-corroboration mechanic / contradiction handling is the
strongest in this survey — fully automatic, non-destructive (soft-delete via temporal closure, not
flag-and-wait-for-human).

---

## 4. Mem0 — agent memory layer (supporting data point, weaker fit)

Sources: [Mem0 "Introducing Memory Decay"](https://mem0.ai/blog/introducing-memory-decay-in-mem0);
community/architecture writeups on Mem0's graph variant conflict resolution.

1. **Rises over time?** No — Mem0's documented "Memory Decay" feature is a **recency-based scaling
   factor for search ranking only** (0.3x–1.5x), and it only dampens stale memories; it does not
   describe scores rising through repeated corroboration. This is included mainly as a contrast:
   it demonstrates a system that explicitly ages confidence *downward*, the mirror image of what
   dark-factory wants (KDD pattern-promotion rises with recurrence).
2. **Handles contradiction?** Partially/architecture-dependent, and honestly the documentation here
   is inconsistent (flagged as such in secondary sources): Mem0's current default ("ADD-only")
   architecture keeps both old and contradicting memories and defers resolution to retrieval-time
   ranking, rather than resolving at write time. The separate graph variant (Mem0ᵍ) adds an explicit
   Conflict Detector + Update Resolver that can merge, invalidate, or skip conflicting nodes/edges,
   applying a temporal-precedence rule ("recent overwrites historical"). Could not fully verify
   which behavior ships by default vs. is graph-only — flagging this as **unverified in full
   mechanics**, unlike Zep/Kantara where the mechanism is spelled out in a paper.

**Verdict:** confidence does not rise (decays only) / contradiction handling exists but mechanics are
under-documented and reportedly inconsistent between docs and shipped behavior — weakest-verified
example here, included for contrast rather than as a strong model to copy.

---

## Answering the two questions directly, across all examples

| System | Confidence rises with corroboration? | Contradiction handling |
|---|---|---|
| NELL | Yes — coupled learners corroborate candidates up to a promotion threshold | No automatic mechanism; manual human pruning sweeps |
| Kantara/DRMiner | No — static, one-shot LLM extraction per commit | Yes — automatic NLI-based `contradicts` edge (flag only, no auto-supersede) |
| Zep/Graphiti | Not documented as rising; more dedupe-or-invalidate than a climbing score | Yes — fully automatic, closes the earlier fact's validity window (`t_invalid`), keeps history, non-destructive |
| Mem0 | No — decays downward with staleness, does not climb | Partial/unclear — ADD-only default defers to retrieval ranking; graph variant has an explicit resolver (mechanics not fully confirmed) |

Only NELL documents confidence *rising* through corroboration, and even there it's a promotion
threshold crossed via multiple independent extractors agreeing, not a smooth "recurrence_count"
like dark-factory's pattern-promotion. **No system found combines both** a rising corroboration
score *and* a fully automatic contradiction-driven demotion/supersede in one mechanism — the two
halves of the ask live in different systems (NELL has the rise, Zep has the contradiction handling,
Kantara has the closest data shape).

---

## Relevance to dark-factory

Dark-factory's KDD/Lisa flow already independently reinvented NELL's core idea for *learnings*:
recurrence across 3+ sessions promotes a learning to a pattern with a `recurrence_count` — this is
exactly NELL's candidate→belief promotion-by-corroboration mechanic, just simpler (count threshold
vs. multi-extractor voting). That part is validated prior art; no need to change it.

What's missing, mapped to what's proven above:

- **For confidence rising on decisions** — copy NELL's shape almost directly: a `corroboration_count`
  (or reuse `recurrence_count`) on a decision record that increments each time a later session's
  content restates/reinforces it, with a promotion threshold (e.g. "3 corroborating sessions → mark
  confidence: high") rather than a single author-asserted `low/medium/high` (Lore's static
  self-assessment, which this ticket already ruled out as the weak baseline).
- **For contradiction handling** — Zep's mechanic is the best fit to steal, adapted from graph edges
  to markdown decision records: don't delete or silently overwrite a decision when a later session
  contradicts it. Instead, **close its validity window** (add a `superseded_at`/`contradicted_at`
  timestamp + pointer to the contradicting session, leave the original record in place) and open a
  new record — giving Lisa/KDD a full audit trail of "what we believed true when," which matches the
  existing KDD/git-trail philosophy (append-only, provenance-preserving) far better than in-place
  editing would.
- **Detection mechanism** — Kantara/DRMiner's approach (NLI-style semantic contradiction check between
  a new decision and existing ones, thresholded, auto-flagged for human review rather than
  auto-resolved) is the most directly transferable *detection* method for a text-based decision
  corpus like dark-factory's markdown KDD records — closer to David's actual data shape (prose
  decisions in markdown) than Zep's RDF-style edges are. Recommendation: **flag, don't
  auto-supersede** — every system that attempted full automation here (Zep) operates over structured
  graph edges with a clear precedence rule (newest wins); dark-factory's decisions are prose,
  where auto-resolution risk is higher, so Kantara's "detect + flag for human verification" posture
  is the safer starting point, with Zep's non-destructive versioning as the storage pattern
  underneath it.
notes: Knowledge Vault (Google) was also investigated as a candidate but is NOT included as one of the 2-3 headline examples: its PDF paper could not be fetched as readable text (binary/compressed stream errors on two attempts), so its confidence-fusion mechanics (Platt-scaling calibration, Bayesian truth discovery across conflicting extractor sources) are reported only via secondary summaries, not verified against the primary source directly -- flagging per the ticket's 'only count examples where scoring mechanics are actually documented' instruction. Secondary sources suggest Knowledge Vault DOES have a corroboration-like fusion mechanic (more independent extractors agreeing -> higher fused confidence via supervised ML) and a stated 'policy to remove conflicting facts' via iterative Bayesian source-dependence detection, which would make it a 5th relevant example, but mechanics are unverified here -- treat as a lead for follow-up, not a citable answer. Mem0's contradiction-handling mechanics are similarly under-verified: documentation and actual shipped behavior reportedly diverge per a GitHub issue (mem0ai/mem0 #4896), so it is included only as a weak contrast point, not a strong model. Kantara/DRMiner and Zep/Graphiti are the two most solidly verified, most relevant examples (mechanics confirmed via direct paper fetch with quotes); NELL's mechanics are verified via multiple secondary summaries (Wikipedia, AAAI listing) rather than a full primary-paper fetch, but the promotion-threshold and human-curation facts are corroborated across multiple independent sources so confidence is medium-high. Overall confidence: medium -- strong on Kantara and Zep, medium on NELL, weak/unverified on Knowledge Vault and Mem0 contradiction mechanics.
